\begin{document}

\begin{center}
    {\LARGE \bfseries Boundary Value Problems and Sturm--Liouville Theory}\\[6pt]
    {\large Prepared by Staples Education}\\[4pt]
    {\itshape Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4em}

\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Part II --- Review Guide: Practice Problems}
\section*{Part II --- Review Guide: Practice Problems}

Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from telling an IVP from a BVP and solving simple two-point problems, through the full three-case eigenvalue analysis and the shooting method, up to the orthogonality integral, a complete eigenfunction expansion with every coefficient integral expanded, and the Sturm--Liouville orthogonality proof. Solutions follow in Part III.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
    \item \textbf{(IVP vs.\ BVP)} State what distinguishes a boundary value problem from an initial value problem, and identify which of $y(0)=1,\,y'(0)=2$ versus $y(0)=1,\,y(2)=5$ is which.

    \item \textbf{(Simple Dirichlet BVP)} Solve $y''=0$ on $[0,2]$ with $y(0)=1$, $y(2)=5$.

    \item \textbf{(Nonhomogeneous BVP)} Solve $y''=2$ on $[0,1]$ with $y(0)=0$, $y(1)=0$.

    \item \textbf{(Infinitely many solutions)} Show that $y''+y=0$ with $y(0)=0$, $y(\pi)=0$ has infinitely many solutions, and give their form.

    \item \textbf{(Eigenvalues, three cases)} For $y''+\lambda y=0$, $y(0)=y(L)=0$, carry out the $\lambda<0,\ \lambda=0,\ \lambda>0$ analysis and state $\lambda_n$ and $y_n$.

    \item \textbf{(Neumann spectrum)} Find the eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of $y''+\lambda y=0$ with $y'(0)=y'(L)=0$.

    \item \textbf{(Shooting method)} Solve $y''=y$ on $[0,1]$, $y(0)=0$, $y(1)=1$ by two shots and linear interpolation.

    \item \textbf{(Orthogonality integral)} Evaluate $\int_0^L \sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx$ for $m\neq n$ and for $m=n$.

    \item \textbf{(Eigenfunction expansion)} Expand $f(x)=x$ on $[0,L]$ in the sine series $\sum_{n\ge 1}c_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$, computing $c_n$ in full.

    \item \textbf{(Sturm--Liouville capstone)} Write $X''+\lambda X=0$, $X(0)=X(L)=0$ in S--L form (name $p,q,w$), then prove $\int_a^b y_n y_m w\,dx=0$ for $\lambda_n\neq\lambda_m$.

\end{enumerate}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Part III --- Complete Solutions}
\section*{Part III --- Complete Solutions}

\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad A BVP imposes its side conditions at \emph{two different points}, whereas an IVP stacks all of them at a single point. Hence
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=1,\ y'(0)=2 \ \text{(both at } x=0\text{)} \Rightarrow \textbf{IVP}; \qquad y(0)=1,\ y(2)=5 \ \text{(at } x=0,2\text{)} \Rightarrow \textbf{BVP}.} \]
Unlike the IVP, the BVP carries no automatic existence--uniqueness guarantee.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad The general solution of $y''=0$ is $y=c_1+c_2 x$. Imposing the endpoints,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=c_1=1; \qquad y(2)=1+2c_2=5 \Rightarrow c_2=2.} \]
Unique solution: $y(x)=1+2x$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad Integrate twice: $y=x^2+c_2 x+c_1$. Then
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=c_1=0; \qquad y(1)=1+c_2=0 \Rightarrow c_2=-1.} \]
Unique solution: $y(x)=x^2-x$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad The general solution is $y=A\cos x+B\sin x$. The conditions give
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=A=0; \qquad y(\pi)=A\cos\pi+B\sin\pi=-A=0,} \]
which is satisfied for \emph{every} $B$. Thus $y=B\sin x$ is a solution for all $B$ --- infinitely many (here $\lambda=1$ is an eigenvalue).

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad For $\lambda<0$ and $\lambda=0$ the BCs force $y\equiv 0$. For $\lambda=\mu^2>0$, $y=A\cos\mu x+B\sin\mu x$ with $y(0)=A=0$, so $y=B\sin\mu x$ and $y(L)=B\sin\mu L=0$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\sin\mu L=0 \Rightarrow \mu L=n\pi \Rightarrow \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\quad y_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L},\quad n=1,2,3,\dots} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad With $y=A\cos\mu x+B\sin\mu x$, $y'=\mu(-A\sin\mu x+B\cos\mu x)$. Then $y'(0)=\mu B=0\Rightarrow B=0$, and $y'(L)=-\mu A\sin\mu L=0$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\sin\mu L=0 \Rightarrow \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\quad y_n=\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L},\quad n=0,1,2,\dots} \]
The Neumann case keeps $n=0$: $\lambda_0=0$ with constant eigenfunction $y_0=1$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad Recast as $y''=y$, $y(0)=0$, $y'(0)=s$, with exact IVP solution $y(x;s)=s\sinh x$. Two shots:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{F(0)=0-1=-1; \quad F(1)=\sinh 1-1\approx 0.1752; \quad s^\star=\frac{1}{\sinh 1}\approx 0.8509.} \]
Final solution: $y(x)=\dfrac{\sinh x}{\sinh 1}$, exact since the problem is linear.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quad Use $\sin\alpha\sin\beta=\tfrac12[\cos(\alpha-\beta)-\cos(\alpha+\beta)]$. For $m\neq n$ both cosine integrals vanish over $[0,L]$, giving $0$. For $m=n$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\int_0^L \sin^2\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\int_0^L\frac{1-\cos\frac{2n\pi x}{L}}{2}\,dx=\frac{L}{2}.} \]
So the integral is $0$ for $m\neq n$ and $L/2$ for $m=n$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad With weight $w=1$, $c_n=\big(\int_0^L x\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}dx\big)\big/\big(\int_0^L\sin^2\frac{n\pi x}{L}dx\big)$. The denominator is $L/2$; integrating the numerator by parts,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\int_0^L x\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\Big[-\frac{Lx}{n\pi}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big]_0^L+\frac{L}{n\pi}\int_0^L\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{L^2}{n\pi}+0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{c_n=\frac{(-1)^{n+1}L^2/(n\pi)}{L/2}=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2L}{n\pi} \ \Rightarrow\ x=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2L}{n\pi}\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}.} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad Matching $(pX')'+(q+\lambda w)X=0$ to $X''+\lambda X=0$ gives $p=1,\ q=0,\ w=1$ (regular, since $p,w>0$ with separated BCs). For the proof, multiply $(py_n')'+\lambda_n w y_n=0$ by $y_m$, the $m$-equation by $y_n$, subtract, and integrate:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{(\lambda_n-\lambda_m)\int_a^b w\,y_n y_m\,dx=\big[p\,(y_n y_m'-y_m y_n')\big]_a^b=0,} \]
where the bracket vanishes by the homogeneous BCs. Since $\lambda_n\neq\lambda_m$, $\int_a^b y_n y_m w\,dx=0$.

\medskip

\begin{conceptbox}[Unit Key Takeaway]
One thread ties the unit together: \textcolor{accentorange}{boundary conditions create a spectrum, and orthogonality turns that spectrum into a basis}. Splitting the side data across two endpoints can leave a linear problem with no solution, one, or infinitely many --- the last case is where the \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenvalues $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$} and \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenfunctions $\sin(n\pi x/L)$} live. Those eigenfunctions are \textcolor{accentorange}{orthogonal with respect to a weight $w$}, so any reasonable $f$ expands as $f=\sum c_n y_n$ with each $c_n=\langle f,y_n\rangle/\langle y_n,y_n\rangle$ a single integral. The \textcolor{accentorange}{Sturm--Liouville form $(p\,y')'+(q+\lambda w)y=0$} is the master frame that guarantees real eigenvalues, weighted orthogonality, and a complete functional basis.
\end{conceptbox}

\end{document}
